title: Parallel lines
source: Essential Mathematics for the Australian Curriculum, Year 9 (Cambridge, Greenwood et al. 2024) — Chapter 7 Geometry, Section 7B. Images are direct crops of the original textbook pages.

workedExamples.title: Example 4 — Deciding if lines are parallel
Example 5 — Finding angles in parallel lines
Example 6 — Adding a third parallel line

questions.topic: parallel line angle rules (fill in the blank)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): equal

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: treating a cointerior (supplementary) pair as if it were equal, or an alternate/corresponding (equal) pair as if it summed to 180°
misconceptions.reason: corresponding and alternate angles are EQUAL when lines are parallel; cointerior angles are SUPPLEMENTARY (sum to 180°) - mixing these up is the most common error in this topic

questions.answer: ∠ABC = 100°, ∠BCD = 80°, ∠ABC + ∠BCD = 180°, therefore AB ∥ DC as cointerior angles are supplementary.
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠ABC = 100°, ∠BCD = 80°, ∠ABC + ∠BCD = 180°, therefore AB ∥ DC as cointerior angles are supplementary.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: concluding parallel because the angles look similar, without checking they actually sum to 180° or are equal
misconceptions.reason: you must explicitly test the angle relationship (here, that the cointerior pair sums to exactly 180°) before concluding the lines are parallel

questions.topic: using parallel lines to prove the triangle angle sum
questions.answer: Cointerior angles on parallel lines add to 180°.
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): Cointerior angles on parallel lines add to 180°.

questions.topic: using parallel lines to prove the triangle angle sum
questions.answer: Alternate angles are equal, on parallel lines.
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): Alternate angles are equal, on parallel lines.

questions.topic: using parallel lines to prove the triangle angle sum
questions.answer: Since a + b + c = 180 (from parts a and b combined) and a, b, c are exactly the three interior angles of the triangle, this proves the angle sum of any triangle is 180°.
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): Since a + b + c = 180 (from parts a and b combined) and a, b, c are exactly the three interior angles of the triangle, this proves the angle sum of any triangle is 180°.

questions.topic: proof in geometry (constructing an auxiliary parallel line)
questions.answer: ∠BAE = 180° − a° (alternate angles, AB ∥ DE). ∠ABC = 180° − c° − (180° − a°) (angle sum of a triangle) = 180° − c° − 180° + a° = a° − c°.
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠BAE = 180° − a° (alternate angles, AB ∥ DE). ∠ABC = 180° − c° − (180° − a°) (angle sum of a triangle) = 180° − c° − 180° + a° = a° − c°.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: trying to solve directly without adding the extra parallel line
misconceptions.reason: some angles can only be found by first adding a third line parallel to the other two, creating new alternate angle pairs to work with

questions.topic: proof in geometry (constructing an auxiliary parallel line)
questions.answer: ∠ABD = 180° − (a° + b°) (angle sum of triangle ABD). ∠ABC + ∠ABD = 180° (straight line). Therefore ∠ABC = a° + b°.
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠ABD = 180° − (a° + b°) (angle sum of triangle ABD). ∠ABC + ∠ABD = 180° (straight line). Therefore ∠ABC = a° + b°.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: trying to solve directly without adding the extra parallel line
misconceptions.reason: some angles can only be found by first adding a third line parallel to the other two, creating new alternate angle pairs to work with

questions.topic: proof in geometry (constructing an auxiliary parallel line)
questions.answer: Construct XY through B parallel to AE. ∠ABY = a° (alternate angles, AE ∥ XY). ∠CBY = b° (alternate angles, DC ∥ XY). Therefore ∠ABC = a° + b°.
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): Construct XY through B parallel to AE. ∠ABY = a° (alternate angles, AE ∥ XY). ∠CBY = b° (alternate angles, DC ∥ XY). Therefore ∠ABC = a° + b°.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: trying to solve directly without adding the extra parallel line
misconceptions.reason: some angles can only be found by first adding a third line parallel to the other two, creating new alternate angle pairs to work with

questions.topic: proof in geometry (constructing an auxiliary parallel line)
questions.answer: Construct XY through A parallel to ED. ∠XAD = 180° − b° (cointerior angles, ED ∥ XY). ∠DAB = 360° − a° (revolution). So ∠XAB = 360° − a° − (180° − b°) = 180° + b° − a°. ∠ABC = ∠XAB (alternate angles, XY ∥ BC). Therefore ∠ABC = 180° + b° − a°.
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): Construct XY through A parallel to ED. ∠XAD = 180° − b° (cointerior angles, ED ∥ XY). ∠DAB = 360° − a° (revolution). So ∠XAB = 360° − a° − (180° − b°) = 180° + b° − a°. ∠ABC = ∠XAB (alternate angles, XY ∥ BC). Therefore ∠ABC = 180° + b° − a°.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: trying to solve directly without adding the extra parallel line
misconceptions.reason: some angles can only be found by first adding a third line parallel to the other two, creating new alternate angle pairs to work with
